plainMeaning: This is the householder's vow not to steal — not to take anything, living or non-living, that has not been given — and here the reciter searches out the five ways he may have broken it, from buying stolen goods to using false weights and selling fake or adulterated things.
recitation: trījuṁ sthūla adattādāna viramaṇa vrata. sacitta, acitta, rāja-nigraha kāriuṁ, piyāruṁ, aṇadīdhuṁ levā niyama. sūkṣma tṛṇa, indhaṇa pathi patita vyavahāra-nioge dāṇacorī, jayaṇā. e trījā sthūla adattādāna viramaṇa vrata taṇā pāñca atiyāra śodhuṁ. tenāhaḍe, takkarapaoge, viruddha rajjāi kamme, kūḍatulla kūḍamāṇe, tappaḍi rūvaga vavahāre. stenāhata—jāṇī karī corāi vastu līdhī hoya. taskara-prayoga—corane saṁbala āpyuṁ hoya. pagarahaṇa dīdhuṁ hoya, āgala thai corāi vastu veñcī veñcāvī hoya. viruddha-rajjāi kamme—viruddha-rājya jai vyavasāya kīdho hoya. kūḍatulla-kūḍamāṇe—kūḍāṁ tolāṁ kūḍāṁ māpāṁ kīdhāṁ hoya, anere dīdhuṁ anere līdhuṁ hoya, lahaka-ṛhaka kīdhuṁ hoya, kāṭalāṁ kūḍāṁ kīdhāṁ hoya, usisī dīdhī hoya. tat-pratirūpaka-vyavahāra—sapharī vastu-māṁhe napharī vastu ghālī sapharīne mūlye veñcī hoya. ghī, tela, sāhilī, kaucī bhelasaṁbhela kīdhāṁ hoya. navī vānakī dekhāḍī jūnī vastu āpī hoya. tathā kūḍo karaho kāḍhyo hoya, vañca-droha, viśvāsa-ghāta kīdho hoya. lekhe koya varāśyuṁ hoya, kūḍī mudrā saṁcārī hoya. sisīsī javāra, phalyā caṇā upāḍyā hoya, kuṇaha-nuṁ dravya vyavasāya karatāṁ āpaṇāpāṁ kane rahyuṁ hoya, nāṇāṁ palaṭo kīdho hoya. aneruṁ e trījā sthūla adattādāna viramaṇa-vrata viṣe.

literalTranslation: Third: the vow of abstaining from gross taking of what is not given. My rule is not to take — whether ensouled or soulless, whether a thing whose theft brings state-punishment, or a cherished thing — anything not given. As for subtle cases — a blade of grass, fuel fallen on the road, or evasions arising in the ordinary course of dealings — there is jayaṇā (guarding-care). I now examine the five transgressions of this third vow of abstaining from gross theft: (1) tenāhaḍe — receiving stolen goods; (2) takkarapaoge — employing/abetting a thief; (3) viruddha-rajjāi-kamme — trading against the law of the realm; (4) kūḍatulla-kūḍamāṇe — false weights and measures; (5) tappaḍirūvaga-vavahāre — dealing in counterfeit look-alikes. [Glosses:] Knowing a thing to be stolen, I may have taken it. I may have given a thief provisions, given him shelter, or, acting as his front, sold or got stolen goods sold. I may have done business by going into a hostile/contraband realm. I may have kept false weights and false measures — giving by one and taking by another, doing light-and-heavy trickery, keeping false weight-stones, skimming the heap. I may have inserted inferior goods into superior and sold them at the superior price; adulterated ghee, oil, sāhilī, kaucī; shown a fine sample and delivered old stock; drawn up a false account, committed fraud and breach of trust; short-changed someone in the reckoning, passed false coin; carried off another's millet or ripening gram; while trading, kept another's wealth with myself; worked a money-swap fraud. Whatever else there was regarding this third vow of abstaining from gross theft [— may that wrong of mine be undone].
idiomaticTranslation: Third comes the householder's vow against gross theft. My resolve is to take nothing that is not freely given — be it living or lifeless, a cherished possession or goods the state itself protects. For the smallest unavoidable things — a wisp of grass, fuel dropped on the road, the petty frictions of honest trade — I rely on watchful care (jayaṇā). And now I search my conduct for the five ways this vow may have slipped: handling stolen goods, abetting a thief, trafficking against the law of the land, cheating with false weights and measures, and trading in counterfeits. Did I knowingly buy what was stolen? Did I provision a thief, shelter him, or play the fence who moves his loot? Did I deal in contraband across hostile borders? Did I rig my scales and measures — give by one, take by another, palm the light for the heavy, keep crooked weight-stones, shave the heap? Did I slip cheap goods into fine and charge the fine price; adulterate ghee, oil and grain; show a bright sample but hand over old stock; cook a false account; betray a trust; short a man in his reckoning; pass forged coin; carry off another's standing crop of millet or gram; pocket a client's money during a deal; or work a sleight in changing cash? For all this, and whatever else against this third vow — may my wrongdoing be undone.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having confessed false speech in the second vow, the reciter now turns from the tongue to the hand: adattādāna, 'taking what is not given.' The verse first fixes the vow's scope with two exhaustive pairs — sacitta/acitta (living/non-living) and the contrast of an everyday 'cherished thing' (piyāruṁ) with goods whose theft draws state penalty (rāja-nigraha) — so that no category of property escapes the resolve. Crucially, it then carves out jayaṇā: a blade of grass, fuel lying fallen on the road, the inevitable frictions of vyavahāra are not declared sins but guarded with care — the lay alternative to a monk's absolute asteya. The heart of the verse is the canonical Prakrit pentad — tenāhaḍe, takkarapaoge, viruddha-rajjāi-kamme, kūḍatulla-kūḍamāṇe, tappaḍirūvaga-vavahāre — which the medieval-Gujarati gloss then unfolds into a strikingly concrete bazaar of dishonesty. What gives this verse its force is its insistence that theft is far wider than burglary: the receiver, the harbourer, the fence, the smuggler, the shopkeeper with two sets of weights, the adulterator of ghee and oil, the seller who shows one sample and delivers another, the forger of coin and account, the money-changer's sleight, even pocketing a client's cash mid-deal — all are adattādāna. The catalogue moves deliberately from the obvious thief outward to the respectable merchant, refusing the householder any comfortable distance from the vice. The sealing 'aneruṁ … viṣe' gathers every unnamed variant into the shared micchā-mi-dukkaḍaṁ. (A few trade-terms — sāhilī, kaucī, lahaka-ṛhaka, usisī, varāśyuṁ — are dialectal and their exact referents uncertain; see notes.)
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The five glosses align exactly with the classical five atichāras of the asteya-aṇuvrata as fixed in Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.27 (stena-prayoga, tadāhṛtādāna, viruddha-rājyātikrama, hīnādhika-mānonmāna, pratirūpaka-vyavahāra), showing this medieval confession transmitting the canonical list faithfully under its vernacular names. Doctrinally the verse enforces the Jain principle that theft is not only the act but its abetment and its disguises: prayoga (instigation) and anumodanā (approval/profiting) bind karma as surely as the deed, which is why receiving, harbouring, and fencing are named first. The weights-and-measures and counterfeit clauses extend asteya into commercial honesty (vyavahāra-śuddhi), making fair trade a spiritual discipline, not mere social ethic — every false weight is a covert taking of 'what is not given.' Underlying all is aparigraha's near neighbour: grasping more than one's due is itself a form of hiṁsā against trust and against one's own right faith.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For a modern reciter this verse is an audit of working life: it asks not 'did I rob anyone?' but 'did I cut a corner?' — pirated or stolen goods knowingly used, contraband or tax-evaded dealings, rigged metrics or invoices, adulterated or misrepresented products, bait-and-switch samples, withheld client funds, manipulated currency or accounts. Reciting it sincerely turns pratikramaṇa into a ledger-review of one's trade and converts honest measure, accurate billing, and refusal to profit from another's theft into daily expressions of the vow.

sources.title: Anuvrata (Śrāvaka) — five lay vows and their atichāras
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the third aṇuvrata (acaurya/asteya) and its place among the five lay vows and six āvaśyakas.

sources.title: Asteya / Achaurya — non-stealing in Jainism
sources.consultedFor: Definition and scope of asteya (act, speech, thought) underpinning the vow's framing.

sources.title: Asteya — Sanskrit/Prakrit lexical and Jain definitions
sources.consultedFor: Etymology (a- + steya) and cross-tradition senses used in the word-by-word glosses.

sources.title: Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.27 — atichāras of the asteya-aṇuvrata
sources.consultedFor: Aligning the verse's five Prakrit atichāra-names with the canonical five (stena-prayoga, tadāhṛtādāna, viruddha-rājyātikrama, hīnādhika-mānonmāna, pratirūpaka-vyavahāra).

notes: The canonical five-fold structure and the four named heads (receiving stolen goods, abetting a thief, unlawful-realm trade, false weights/measures, counterfeit dealing) are secure and match Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.27. Lower confidence attaches to several medieval-Gujarati trade/dialect terms whose exact referents are uncertain and are rendered by contextual sense: 'lahaka-ṛhaka' (light/heavy weighing trickery), 'usisī' (skimming/shorting a heap), 'sapharī'/'napharī' (superior vs inferior grade goods), 'sāhilī' and 'kaucī' (two adulterable commodities, likely grains/pulses or refined sugar), 'karaho' (a false account/levy), 'varāśyuṁ' (to dupe/short-change in reckoning), and 'sisīsī' (a grain-measure or pilfering term). The book's verbatim orthography is preserved (e.g. 'atiyār' for atichāra; spaced/period punctuation; 'pagarahaṇa'). The sealing formula is abbreviated here to 'aneruṁ … viṣe'; the full micchā-mi-dukkaḍaṁ seal is the sutra-level refrain noted in the preface and not re-glossed.
